# Quine

> Quine is a content-agnostic, real-time 3D simulation engine compiled to
> WebAssembly. It renders **only the scene it is handed** and the meshes the host
> provides at boot — it ships with no game content, no menus, and no UI. Hosts
> (a web page, the editor, a native app) load the engine bundle, feed it a scene
> as data, and composite any HTML overlay on top.

The engine is built on one hard rule: **data flows core → render, never back.**
A headless, deterministic core advances the simulation by a fixed timestep; a
render layer reads that state and draws it but never mutates it. The core runs
windowless (CI, replay, server) with identical results. Because the engine knows
nothing about specific worlds, the same wasm renders every scene; content lives
in data (scene JSON), behaviour in skills (sandboxed JS), and UI in overlays
(plain HTML) — all supplied by the host, none baked into the engine.

Distribution is the CDN's job: engine bundles and shared assets are served from
`https://cdn.qubeworlds.com` (`/engine/quine-{webgl2,webgpu}.{js,wasm}`). Apps
reference those URLs; they do not bundle the engine.

## Core concepts

- [Scenes are data](https://github.com/qubeworlds/quine/blob/main/modules/core/scene.zig): A scene is JSON — a list of `entities`, each carrying components (`transform`, `geometry`, `material`, `camera`, `light`, `body`, `spin`, …). Unknown fields are ignored (forward-compatible). The engine builds the scene's entities and renders them; it never authors content.
- [Fixed timestep, decoupled from render](https://github.com/qubeworlds/quine/blob/main/apps/desktop/main.zig): The host advances the sim in fixed `1/fixedHz`-second steps drained from a wall-clock accumulator, independent of render frame rate. A scene sets its rate with `"fixedHz": 64` (default 60) — the `Time::<Fixed>::from_hz` analogue. Set `"interpolate": true` to have the renderer lerp transforms between sim ticks by the accumulator's overstep fraction, so a sim rate that doesn't divide the display refresh stays smooth.
- [Skills are behaviour](https://github.com/qubeworlds/quine/blob/main/modules/script/prelude.js): A scene may link a `script` (a sandboxed QuickJS skill). It runs *inside* the fixed step via `onPreStep(dt)` / `onPostStep()`, reading and writing the live world through a small facade: `world.get(name).transform.position` / `.rotation`, `.body.velocity`, `.material.emissive`, `world.contactImpulse(a,b)`, `audio.bus/sfx`, and `input(axis)`. The skill owns game logic; the engine stays generic.
- [Input is a device-agnostic axis bus](https://github.com/qubeworlds/quine/blob/main/modules/script/script.zig): The engine exposes 8 float input axes a skill reads via `input(axis)`. The engine knows nothing about keyboards, touch, or gamepads — the **host** maps physical devices onto axes by enqueuing `{ "type": "input", "axis": N, "value": v }`. On the web this is owned by an overlay (see below).
- [Scenes link a reusable HTML overlay](https://github.com/qubeworlds/world/blob/main/overlays/navigator.js): A scene's optional `"overlay": "<url>"` points at a reusable, hydrating HTML module exporting `mount(container, host) → { unmount() }`. The engine ignores it; the host mounts it over the `<canvas>` and composites. Overlays are where menus, navigation (`navigator.js`), and on-screen game controls (`controls.js`, which maps touch + keys onto the input axes) live — never engine code.

## Loading the engine

- [Run it live](https://quine.qubeworlds.com/): The runtime loader auto-detects WebGPU and falls back to WebGL2 (`?render=webgl|webgpu` to force).
- [Boot sequence + on-page debugger](https://qubeworlds.com/scene): A framework-free harness that loads the engine on one scene in isolation, echoing every `print`/error and a WebGL2-context-exhaustion probe. Use `?url=<scene.json>&autoload=1`. The canonical way to tell an engine/scene bug from a host-integration bug.
- The host boots the engine empty, then feeds it messages in order via `quine_enqueue(json)`: `{type:"config"}`, assets via `quine_provide_asset`, `{type:"scene", json}`, then `{type:"skill", code}`. The web SDK (`mountScene`) wraps this with an OPFS wasm cache, a timed `instantiateWasm`, and single-WebGL-context handoff.

## Source

- [Repository (qubeworlds/quine)](https://github.com/qubeworlds/quine): The engine. Zig, wgpu/sokol, Jolt physics, QuickJS skills.
- [CLAUDE.md — architecture & rules](https://github.com/qubeworlds/quine/blob/main/CLAUDE.md): The core→render rule, the "engine knows nothing about content" rule, the CDN distribution rule, and the build/headless-render commands.
- [render_queue.zig — the render snapshot](https://github.com/qubeworlds/quine/blob/main/modules/core/render_queue.zig): `extract(prev, cur, alpha)` — how the render layer reads (and interpolates) world state.

## Optional

- [The world platform (qubeworlds/world)](https://github.com/qubeworlds/world): The host apps (site, editor, play), the `@world/qubegame` client, and the reusable `overlays/` (navigator, controls). Shows how a host mounts the engine + overlay and renders both.
- [Native desktop app](https://github.com/qubeworlds/quine/blob/main/apps/desktop/main.zig): The reference host — owns the window and the fixed-timestep accumulator; the web build is this same app compiled to `wasm32-emscripten`.
